coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional fish gelatin film incorporated with chitosan carbon dots and butterfly pea flower anthocyanins for active/smart packaging of Pacific white shrimp

coredata.dc:description: A pH-responsive and active film formulated using fish gelatin incorporated with chitosan carbon dots (CDs) and butterfly pea flower (Clitoria ternatea) anthocyanins (BA) was developed and used for the packaging of Pacific white shrimp (Litopenaeus vannamei). CDs showed more effective inhibition zones against Listeria monocytogenes and Staphylococcus aureus compared to Salmonella enterica and Escherichia coli. The addition of CDs (1, 3, and 5 wt%) significantly increased (p < 0.05) the mechanical properties of gelatin film. The film containing BA and 5% CDs showed excellent UV-barrier, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties. The functional films with 5% CD effectively inhibited the growth of L. monocytogenes within 6 h and E. coli within 9 h of incubation, respectively. The gelatin-based films combined with BA and CD showed distinct color changes with different pHs (from 2 to 12), confirming their potential for intelligent packaging purposes. Films containing BA and CD preserved shrimp quality, delayed bacterial growth, and maintained shrimp color more effectively during packaging and storage of Pacific white shrimp at 4 °C for 12 days. On the final day of storage, shrimp kept in the pouches made with 5% CD film showed the lowest total viable count and psychrophilic bacteria count (4.9 and 4.4 log CFU/mL, respectively). Therefore, gelatin-based films containing CD and BA are promising materials for antibacterial packaging films and can detect real-time quality changes in seafood.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional fish gelatin film incorporated with chitosan carbon dots and butterfly pea flower anthocyanins for active/smart packaging of Pacific white shrimp

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A pH-responsive and active film formulated using fish gelatin incorporated with chitosan carbon dots (CDs) and butterfly pea flower (Clitoria ternatea) anthocyanins (BA) was developed and used for the packaging of Pacific white shrimp (Litopenaeus vannamei). CDs showed more effective inhibition zones against Listeria monocytogenes and Staphylococcus aureus compared to Salmonella enterica and Escherichia coli. The addition of CDs (1, 3, and 5 wt%) significantly increased (p < 0.05) the mechanical properties of gelatin film. The film containing BA and 5% CDs showed excellent UV-barrier, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties. The functional films with 5% CD effectively inhibited the growth of L. monocytogenes within 6 h and E. coli within 9 h of incubation, respectively. The gelatin-based films combined with BA and CD showed distinct color changes with different pHs (from 2 to 12), confirming their potential for intelligent packaging purposes. Films containing BA and CD preserved shrimp quality, delayed bacterial growth, and maintained shrimp color more effectively during packaging and storage of Pacific white shrimp at 4 °C for 12 days. On the final day of storage, shrimp kept in the pouches made with 5% CD film showed the lowest total viable count and psychrophilic bacteria count (4.9 and 4.4 log CFU/mL, respectively). Therefore, gelatin-based films containing CD and BA are promising materials for antibacterial packaging films and can detect real-time quality changes in seafood.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Packaging is generally used to protect food from the environment, delay quality deterioration, and increase safety from production to consumption. However, in appropriate packaging can result in spoilage of food or cause food waste, which is responsible for increasing greenhouse gases (Ahmed & Ikram, 2016). Conventional packaging mainly uses disposable and non-degradable plastics, which cause serious environmental problems when discarded after use. Microbial contamination during harvesting, processing, and packaging causes food spoilage and safety issues, contributing to food waste. The main microorganisms behind the spoilage of fresh, processed, and packaged foods are bacteria and molds (Karanth, Feng, Patra, & Pradhan, 2023). Therefore, there is an increasing demand for innovative, environmentally friendly, and active packaging materials that can effectively preserve food (Versino et al., 2023). Currently, new advancements in food packaging are active packaging and intelligent packaging (Cui et al., 2023; Priyadarshi, Ezati, & Rhim, 2021). Active packaging is prepared by mixing active ingredients in the polymer matrix to increase the shelf life of food and guarantee its safety (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023). Intelligent packaging can monitor food quality affected by storage conditions to ensure consumer safety and provide real-time quality information on products to consumers (Khan et al., 2023b). This approach creates a smart packaging system, in which a critical connection between packaging materials, the surrounding environment and food quality is established. Indicative parameters include pH, volatile compounds, temperature, oxygen level, and microbial growth (Priyadarshi, Ezati, & Rhim, 2021). Basically, a pH-sensitive dye indicator was used because it is a simple and non-destructive method. Since pH changes are often associated with alterations in food quality, these pH-dependent color change indicators can enable accurate monitoring of food spoilage (Dodero, Escher, Bertucci, Castellano, & Lova, 2021).

Biodegradable polymers are gaining significant interest in food packaging to replace traditional plastic packaging. Gelatin, a protein derived from collagen that is frequently utilized in the food and pharmaceutical sector, is a widely used biopolymer for film fabrication due to its excellent ability to prevent oxygen permeation (Luo et al., 2022). Because of its edibility, biodegradability, abundance and transparency, gelatin is a widely used biopolymer to create biodegradable films (Gómez-Estaca, Gómez-Guillén, Fernández-Martín, & Montero, 2011; Haghighi et al., 2019; Pattarasiriroj, Kaewprachu, & Rawdkuen, 2020; Rawdkuen, Faseha, Benjakul, & Kaewprachu, 2020). However, its poor water vapor barrier properties limit its commercial use (Karim & Bhat, 2009). To address the limitations of gelatin film, composite films are made by adding active fillers to gelatin (Rivero, García, & Pinotti, 2009). These composite films combine the beneficial properties of gelatin with other materials to provide more effective food packaging.

Anthocyanins, which are part of the flavonoid family, are natural pigments present in colorful fruits, vegetables, and flowers (Mattioli, Francioso, Mosca, & Silva, 2020). They are water-soluble pigments and have stronger antioxidant activity than vitamins E and C (Ibrahim, Mahmoud, Osman, Abd El-Aal, & Eid, 2011). Anthocyanins offer unique advantages, particularly their ability to change color based on the pH of the environment (Khoo, Azlan, Tang, & Lim, 2017). Narayanan, Radhakrishnan, & Baiju, 2023 developed a smart pH indicator film by combining gelatin/pectin with butterfly pea flower anthocyanins to track the freshness of tilapia. The film's color changed from dark blue to green after 7 days of storage at 4 °C, and this change was associated with higher levels of TVB-N and pH in the fish.

Recently, nanomaterials have attracted much attention for their ability to improve the physical and chemical properties of packaging products, such as barrier properties and antibacterial effects (Riahi, Rhim, Bagheri, Pircheraghi, & Lotfali, 2022). Highly water-soluble CDs have emerged as promising active fillers (diameter <10 nm) (Khan, Ezati, Rhim, Kim, & Molaei, 2023). They have photoluminescence, fluorescence sensing, and photocatalytic activity (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023b). CDs exhibited strong antioxidant and antibacterial properties, making them valuable for food preservation (Das et al., 2022). Using agricultural waste as a carbon source for CD production can promote sustainability and reduce environmental pollution (Khan, Ezati, et al., 2023). The potential of CDs as cross-linkers and activators in colorimetric-based indicator systems for monitoring the freshness or quality of food has been noted (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023a).

Thus, the objective of this research was to create a multifunctional film based on fish gelatin film incorporating chitosan CD and butterfly pea flower anthocyanin (BA). For this purpose, gelatin films containing BA and different concentrations of CD were prepared, and the properties were evaluated. Also, the effectiveness of the manufactured film as an effective active/intelligent packaging system was evaluated through the process of packaging Pacific white shrimp for 12 days at 4 °C.

Fish gelatin (FG) powder (200 Bloom, Type A) was purchased from Gel-Tec Co. Ltd. (Seoul, Korea). Chitosan (CS) (MW: 1.9–3.1 × 105 Da, degree of deacetylation: 75%–85%) and all antioxidant chemicals were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Fresh Pacific white shrimp (L. vannamei) was purchased from a local supermarket (Seoul, Korea).

CDs were prepared using a hydrothermal process (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023b). For this, CS powder (2%, w/v) was dispersed in distilled water. The suspension was placed in a Teflon-lined chamber located in a stainless steel reactor and then heated at 200 °C for 6 h in a muffle furnace. After the reaction was complete, the reactor was cooled to room temperature. The resulting yellow solution containing CDs was filtered through 100 μm Whatman filter paper and 0.22 μm membrane filters, respectively. The obtained CDs were then freeze-dried and stored in zip-locked bags at 4 °C for further use and characterization.

Light absorption, in the range of 200–800 nm, was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Mecasys Optizen POP Series UV/Vis, Seoul, Korea). CS-CDs were also subjected to Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic analysis (FTIR, IFS 66 V/S, Bruker, Ettlingen, Germany). The absorption data were collected in 32 scans in the wavenumber range of 4000–500 cm−1 with an ATR platinum crystal at 25 °C. The size and shape of the synthesized CS-CDs were determined using a transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, JEM-2100F, Jeol Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence responses of the CS-CDs were evaluated using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Fluoromax, Horiba Inc., Park Avenue, Edison, NJ), respectively.
The radical scavenging activities (RSA) of CS-CDs against ABTS and DPPH radicals were examined as described by Khan, Ezati and Rhim. (2023). The activities were expressed as % radical scavenged.

The antimicrobial activity of CS-CDs against various bacteria (Listeria monocytogenes, Staphylococcus aureus, Salmonella enteric a , and Escherichia coli) was evaluated following the method of Tagrida and Benjakul. (2022) using agar well diffusion method. Briefly, 0.1 mL of selected bacteria (6 Log CFU/mL) was spread on the surface of agar (90 mm diameter) using a sterilized swab. Wells (8 mm in diameter) were created in agar, and then CS-CD solution (100 μL) at various concentrations (3000 - 6000 ppm) was added to these wells. Before incubation, plates were left for 2 h to promote the diffusion of CS-CD. The zone of inhibition was then measured in diameter (mm). Photos, including clear zones, were also taken.

Anthocyanins were extracted from butterfly pea (C. ternatea) flowers using ultrasound-assisted solvent extraction (Mehmood et al., 2019). Ultrasonic treatment was performed as described by Ponnusamy et al. (2024). The resulting anthocyanin extraction powder was named BA. The total anthocyanin content was 324.45 ± 2.34 mg/g, as determined by the pH differential analysis method (Mary et al., 2020).

Fish gelatin (FG) (2%, w/v) was firstly dissolved in distilled water at 70 °C, followed by the addition of BA (4%, w/v) to the polymer solution at 50 °C with vigorous stirring using a magnetic stirrer, and glycerol (30 wt% based on FG) was added as a plasticizer. CDs of various concentrations (0, 1, 3, and 5 wt% based on FG) were then incorporated into the solution, and the mixture was stirred at 50 °C for 1 h to achieve a homogeneous blend. The film-forming solutions were then cast onto Teflon-lined glass plates (24 cm × 30 cm) and dried for 48 h at room temperature (23 °C ± 2 °C). Dried films were then peeled off and conditioned in a humidity chamber (50% ± 1% RH and 25 °C) for 48 h. The fabricated films were designated as FG film (control), FG/BA: FG with anthocyanin, FG/BA/1%CD, FG/BA/3%CD, and FG/BA/5%CD: FG/BA containing CDs at 1, 3, and 5 wt%, respectively.
The thickness of the film was measured at seven different random positions using a digital micrometer (Mitutoyo Corp., Model no. IDC112PM, Kawasaki-shi, Japan) and expressed as the average value.

Tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EAB) of films were measured using a universal testing machine (UTM, Model 5565, Instron Engineering Corporation, Canton, MA, USA) (Tagrida, Nilsuwan, Gulzar, Prodpran, & Benjakul, 2023). Film samples (150 mm × 10 mm) were prepared using an ASTM standard blade cutter (QMS220802-2) and mounted onto the UTM with an initial grip length of 50 mm. The cross-head speed of the UTM was set at 10 mm/min during the tensile test. The force and displacement applied to the film sample were recorded until the film sample broke. TS and EAB of the film were calculated based on recorded data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP) and water contact angle (WCA)
ce:section.ce:para:
The WVP of the films was determined using the gravimetric method (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023). Film samples (70 mm × 70 mm) were sealed onto a permeation cup comprising 18 mL of deionized water. The initial weight of each cup-film assembly was recorded. The cups were then incubated in a humidity chamber (FX1077, Jeio Tech Co. Ltd., Seoul, South Korea) maintained at 25 °C and 50% RH. The weight of each cup-film assembly was monitored every hour for a total of 9 h. Water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) in g/m2·s was computed (Sul, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023) WVP (g · m-1 · s-1 · Pa-1) was then calculated as follows:
(1)
W V P =
(
W V T R × l
)
Δ p
where l is the average thickness (m), and Δp is the partial water vapor pressure difference (Pa) across the film.

WCA, which represents the surface wettability of the films, was evaluated using a contact angle analyzer (Phoenix 150, Surface Electro-Optics Co., Ltd., South Korea). Film samples (2.5 cm × 10 cm) were secured onto a movable platform. A 10 μL-droplet of distilled water was dispensed onto the surface of the film using a micro syringe. Once the droplet was stabilized, the WCA was measured. WCA for each film sample was examined five times, and the average WCA value was reported.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color, light transmittance, and UV barrier properties
ce:section.ce:para: The color of film samples was measured as detailed by Ezati and Rhim. (2022) using a Konica Minolta chromameter (CR-400 Chromameter, Tokyo, Japan). The values were expressed as L∗, a∗, b∗ and ΔE ∗. Photographs of all the films were captured using smartphone camera.

The light transmission of films was assessed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer across a wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The UV barrier property, including UV-A (320–400 nm) and UV-B (280–320 nm), was computed by calculating the percentage blocking of the UV-A and UV-B light (Khan, Ezati, et al., 2023) as follows:
(2)

where T(λ) is the average transmittance of the film at the specific wavelength λ, and dλ is the bandwidth (wavelength interval) of the film (1 nm).

The surface and cross-sectional morphologies, along with elemental mapping (EDX) of the CS-CD and BA integrated multifunctional FG films, were determined by field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, SU 8010, Hitachi Co., Ltd., S4 Matsuda, Japan) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) (S.No. 56756, Oxford Instruments INCA, UK) at 8 kV accelerating voltage (Khan, Riahi, et al., 2023). For cross-sectional analysis, the films were fractured using liquid nitrogen, coated with gold, and mounted on a bronze stub (Sputter Coater SPI module). An acceleration voltage of 20 kV with 10,000 magnification was employed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Attenuated total reflection-Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) and XPS analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Absorption data were collected using 32 scans in the wavenumber range of 4000–500 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 using an ATR platinum crystal at 25 °C. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed using a Bruker FTIR spectrometer (IFS 66 V/S, Bruker Co., Ettlingen, Germany) equipped with an ATR platinum crystal (Gulzar, Tagrida, Nilsuwan, Prodpran, & Benjakul, 2022). The acquired spectra were normalized to ensure reliable comparisons between samples.

The chemical structure and surface functional groups of the films were determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a Κ-alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Co. Ltd., Waltham, MA, USA).
The antioxidant activity of the films was assessed using ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging assays, as outlined in Section 2.3.2 after cutting the film samples (100 mg) into fine pieces.

The antimicrobial properties of the films were assessed against Escherichia coli and Listeria monocytogenes using the total viable count (TVC) method as outlined by Roy, Ezati, and Rhim (2021). For this, sterilized films (100 mg) with UV irradiation for 20 min were suspended in a culture medium containing target microorganisms (1 × 106 CFU/mL), incubated at 37 °C for 18–24 h, and TVC was measured and reported as cell viability (log CFU/mL). All the glassware and utensils used were sterilized by autoclaving at 121 °C for 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Indicator characteristics of multifunctional packaging film

ce:section.ce:para: Film samples (1 cm × 1 cm) were cut and soaked in different buffers with pHs ranging from 2.0 to 12.0 for 1 min. Color changes were determined as specified in Section 2.6.3. Film images were captured using a smartphone camera to observe color changes visually.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity to ammonia and acid vapors
ce:section.ce:para: The response of films containing BA to ammonia and acid vapors was evaluated (Ezati & Rhim, 2020). The test was performed using a beaker (diameter: 40 mm) containing 20 mL of 2.0 M ammonia or 17.4 M acetic acid solution. The tested film was sealed on top of the beaker, with the film positioned 2.5 cm above the solution. This color change reflects the indicator's response to ammonia and acetic acid vapors. Color changes and visual observations of the films were recorded as described in Section 2.7.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Use of film as spoilage indicator
ce:section.ce:para: The real-time detection function of FG/BA/CD films to indicate shrimp spoilage was tested according to the method of Khan, Ezati, et al. (2023). Film samples (20 mm × 20 mm) were glued to the lids of UV-sterilized containers (100 mm × 100 mm × 45 mm). Fresh shrimp (∼50 g) was added to each container, sealed, and incubated at 28 °C in an incubator to accelerate spoilage (Khan, Ezati, et al., 2023). Color changes in the films were examined using a colorimeter at 0, 12, and 24 h.

The effectiveness of the CDs incorporated films in maintaining the shrimp quality was assessed. Shrimp (3 pieces) were packaged in pouches (70 mm × 70 mm) made of each FG-based film. Control samples were packaged in polyethylene (PE) film. Air in all pouches was manually removed before sealing using a heat sealer and stored at 4 °C for 12 days. Throughout the storage period, pH, TBARS values, total viable count (TVC), and psychrophilic bacterial count (PBC) of the samples were evaluated at 3-day intervals.

pH was measured using a pH meter (Cyberscan model 500, Euteon Instruments, Crescent, Singapore). Two grams of shrimp meat were homogenized with 10 mL of distilled water at 8000 rpm for 2 min using a high-speed homogenizer, and the pH of the resulting homogenate was recorded. The TBARS value of the shrimps was determined according to the method of Benjakul and Bauer. (2001) and calculated based on a standard curve of malondialdehyde (MDA) prepared with 1,1,3,3-tetramethoxypropane in the range of 0–10 mg/L. The TBARS values were expressed in mg MDA equivalent per kg of sample.

Shrimp samples packed in pouches were evaluated for total viable count (TVC) and psychrophilic bacterial count (PBC) following the method of Olatunde, Benjakul, and Vongkamjan (2019). Briefly, 5 g of shrimp samples were homogenized with 45 mL of 0.1% peptone water in a stomacher (BagMixer® 400, Interscience, France) for 180 s to obtain a uniform suspension. Serial dilutions of the homogenate were performed, and 1 mL of aliquot from each dilution was plated onto 3M™ Petrifilm counting plates and incubated at 37 °C for TVC (total viable count) for 48 h and 4 °C for PBC (psychrophilic bacteria count) for 7 days. After incubation, the resulting colonies were counted and expressed as Log CFU/g of shrimp.

A completely randomized design was employed for the entire studies. Experiments and analyses were conducted in triplicate. Each film property was measured in triplicate, and the antioxidant and antimicrobial tests were also performed in triplicate using independently prepared film samples as the experimental units. The results were reported as the mean ± standard deviation. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed, and the mean values were compared using Duncan's multiple range test with a significance level of p < 0.05. Statistical analysis was carried out using SPSS software (Statistical Package for Social Science, IBM, IL, USA).

The main absorption peak at 265 nm and a minor shoulder peak at 295 nm in the UV–vis light absorption spectrum of the CDs solution (Fig. 1 a) aligned well with findings from CDs made from green tea (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023b). These peaks are linked to particular electronic transitions in the CD structure. Sharp peak at 265 nm is associated with π-π∗ transitions (-C=C-), and the peak at 295 nm corresponds to n-π∗ transitions (-C=O-) (Roy et al., 2021). The CD solution emitted strong blue fluorescence when exposed to UV light, indicating that the CDs can absorb UV light and emit it in the visible range. This blue emission is characteristic of biomass-based CDs, and the emission intensity can be influenced by the synthesis method and starting materials (Ezati & Rhim, 2022). Similar to other CDs, from other sources, CDs displayed significant UV absorption and decreased absorption in the visible region (Hasan, Saha, Quaid, & Reza, 2021).

The characteristic IR peaks of CS are displayed in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1b), which includes 3367 cm−1 (O-H group), 2989 cm−1 (C-H symmetric stretching), 2904 cm−1 (C-H asymmetric stretching), 1604 cm−1 (C=N; Schiff base), 1370 cm−1 (C-N stretching of amide III), and 1067 cm−1 (C=O group) (Kumar & Koh, 2012). The CDs showed a peak at 3221 cm−1, corresponding to O-H molecules bound to the CDs. Distinct absorption peaks at 1581 and 1463 cm−1, ascribing to N-H bending (primary amine) and CH2 bending, respectively, were found. The peak intensity corresponding to primary amine showed a significant increase in CDs compared to native CS, consistent with the structural alterations in CS after the hydrothermal reaction in which the N-H groups were exposed. The polar functional groups on the surface of the CDs were increased, leading to a hydrophilic nature (Janus, Piątkowski, Radwan-Pragłowska, Bogdał, & Matysek, 2019). This structural deformation of the CS at the molecular level is responsible for the enhanced functional properties (Hafsa et al., 2021). The microstructure and morphology of CDs were observed through TEM analysis (Fig. 1c). CS-CDs were uniformly spherical and monodispersed. The Gaussian model was used to fit the size distribution histogram of the CDs as shown in Fig. 1c (inset). The size of CDs varied from 5 to 15 nm, confirming successful CD synthesis (Roy & Rhim, 2021).

Fluorescent emission spectra were recorded at different excitation wavelengths from 280 to 500 nm in 10 nm intervals (Fig. 1d). When excited at 340 nm, the CDs showed a prominent fluorescent peak between 350 and 600 nm range, reaching its highest emission at 416 nm (Fig. 1d, inset). The emission peaks exhibited a consistent shift towards higher wavelengths as the excitation wavelength increased from 320 to 460 nm, indicating a continuous red shift in the wavelength intensity (Chen et al., 2023). The tunable emission behavior of CDs may be attributed to different factors such as the starting carbon material, method of synthesis, alterations in size and shape, and the types of functional groups on the CD surface (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023).

Antioxidant activities of the CDs determined as ABTS and DPPH-RSA are shown in Fig. 2 a. As the concentration of CDs increased, the ABTS and DPPH-RSA upsurged. The CDs at 100 ppm scavenged nearly 100% of ABTS radicals and 85% of DPPH radicals. The higher number of functional groups upsurged the availability of electron-rich -NH2, -OH and -COOH groups on the surface of CDs. Therefore, the ABTS•+ and DPPH• radicals were better neutralized as a result of the transfer of H• from -NH2, -COOH, and -OH groups to DPPH-H and ABTS-H (Khan, Ezati, et al., 2023; Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, & Wang, 2023). Also, the CDs exhibited greater RSA in the ABTS assay compared to the DPPH assay, which is attributed to the improved solubility of CDs in ABTS aqueous solution resulting from the hydrophilic nature of CDs stemming from the presence of surface -OH groups on the CDs (Khan, Priyadarshi, et al., 2023).

The antimicrobial property of CDs was evaluated on four bacteria, including Listeria monocytogenes, Salmonella enterica, Staphylococcus aureus, and Escherichia coli, using agar well diffusion (Fig. 2b). The CDs showed greater inhibition zones against L. monocytogenes and S. aureus compared to S. enterica and E. coli. The Gram-positive bacteria (GPB) had a larger zone of inhibition than the Gram-negative bacteria (GNB), possibly because of variations in the structure of the bacterial cell wall. The former have a thick peptidoglycan layer with exposed peptide sequences, while the latter have a double layer of lipopolysaccharide over thin peptidoglycan layer in their cell walls, which restricts the pentration of hydrophilic CDs into the cell wall (Rajagopal & Walker, 2015). The small size of CDs helps it penetrate deeper into the cell wall of GPB. In GNB, the presence of a three-layer outer membrane in the cell wall may impede the diffusion or penetration of CDs, thereby reducing the inhibition zone. It has been suggested that the antibacterial efficacy of CDs is related to inhibition of enzyme synthesis and disruption of important cellular processes, resulting in cell death (Zhao et al., 2023).

Moreover, due to their compact size, CDs can easily enter cell walls and interfere with key cellular components crucial for protein and nucleic acid synthesis (Sheta, El-Sheikh, & Abd-Elzaher, 2018). Additionally, the CDs have the potential to trigger the generation of reactive oxygen species that damage cellular structures, ultimately resulting in cell death (Beg et al., 2017; Zheng et al., 2022). The antibacterial property of CDs is consistent with the antibacterial results of CDs prepared from chitosan (Riahi et al., 2022).

The thickness of FG-film increased (p < 0.05) when BA and CDs were added (Table 1 ), resulting from the film's higher solid content (Min, Ezati, & Rhim, 2022). Nonetheless, the thickness of the composite films did not show a significant difference (p > 0.05) because of the small size and hydrophilic characteristic of CDs, which facilitated the distribution of added fillers throughout the FG film matrix (Riahi et al., 2022). Perera, Jaiswal, and Jaiswal (2023) emphasized that film thickness significantly affects the mechanical strength, solubility, moisture and gas barrier properties of packaging films.

The incorporation of BA and CD into FG films at different concentrations affected the mechanical properties like TS and EAB differently. The changes were potentially attributed to their distribution and compatibility within the polymer structure. Differences in TS were obtained between all films except those with 1% and 3% CD addition (Table 1). The FG film with 5% CDs showed the highest TS of 36.2 MPa. Compatible interactions between FG, BA, and CD favored the ordered arrangement of the network, resulting in improved mechanical resistance. Increasing CD concentration resulted in structural integrity within the nanocomposite FG film matrix. Compatibility of film components generally produces a stable film network (Khan, Priyadarshi, et al., 2023). However, the films' EAB increased up to 1% CD, whereas it declined for 3% and 5% CD incorporation. Glycerol acts as a plasticizer, enhancing film flexibility by disrupting interactions between gelatin polymer chains (Fernández-d’Arlas, 2019). The presence of BA could enhance EAB by interfering with the interactions between gelatin chains. These results were similar to those of FG composite films containing epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG). With similar trends, TS increased from 29.9 MPa to 40.7 MPa, while EAB dropped from 24.1% to 7.3% upon the introduction of EGCG (Nilsuwan, Benjakul, Prodpran, & de la Caba, 2019). The EAB of the films decreased significantly (p < 0.05) when the concentration of CD exceeded 1% because intermolecular hydrogen bonds were formed between the surface functionalized groups of CDs and other film components, leading to a strong network structure that reduced EAB. The inclusion of nano-fillers above a certain level increases mechanical strength, depending on their distribution within the film network (Jamróz, Kulawik, & Kopel, 2019). Previously, carboxymethyl cellulose films containing CDs were shown to have increased TS and EAB because of the even dispersal of CDs in the polymer matrix (Riahi et al., 2022). Therefore, 1% CD was found to increase the EAB of the film most effectively.

The WVP and WCA of the FG-based films were measured to assess their water vapor barrier characteristics and surface hydrophilicity (Table 1). The WVP of FG-film was 9.47 ± 0.03 × 10−10 g m−1s−1Pa−1 and significantly decreased (p < 0.05) with the addition of BA and BA combined with CDs. Films with BA and 3% CDs showed the lowest WVP (9.03 ± 0.07 × 10−10 g m−1s−1Pa−1). The addition of CD significantly decreased the WVP of the FG film (p < 0.05), and the decrease was greater as the concentration increased. The integration resulted in a more uniform distribution of CDs and increased interfacial interactions between polymer matrices, resulting in films with a more compact structure and less water penetration, which reduced WVP.

FG film exhibited a WCA of 65.1°, suggesting that it had a hydrophilic surface. The water contact angle of the film rose when BA and CD were added, and the extent of the rise was directly related to the amount of CD added. The higher WCA of the CD-added films may be due to the uniform distribution of CDs in the polymer matrix and their interaction between film components, which decreased the availability of -OH groups in the polymer structure, leading to increased hydrophobicity and thus WCA values (Zabihollahi, Alizadeh, Almasi, Hanifian, & Hamishekar, 2020). The enhanced WCA value of the composite film corresponded well with the reduction in the WVP value due to the inclusion of CDs. Among the film samples, films containing BA, along with 3% and 5% CS-CDs, showed the highest WCA. Films with improved water vapor barrier properties were anticipated to prolong the shelf life of foods by restricting the movement of moisture between the food and the external packaging (Yadav, Mehrotra, Bhartiya, Singh, & Dutta, 2020).

The FG film was transparent with a lightness value (L∗) of 90.88 (Table 2 ), which was in line with the previous report for FG film (Nilsuwan et al., 2019). For films containing BA and CDs, the L∗ value decreased significantly (p < 0.05) because of the intense blue color from BA. The a∗ values of FG films containing BA and CDs varied significantly (p < 0.05) except for the film containing 5% CDs, which showed similar a∗ values as FG films. The b∗ values of the films decreased when the films contained only BA or 1% CDs.

On the other hand, the film containing 5% CDs showed a sharp increase in yellowness (b∗ = 14.08), predominantly caused by the tan color of the CDs. The ΔE ∗ of the films increased from 5.34 to values ranging from 37.19 to 51.26 with BA alone or BA combined with CDs. Khan, Ezati, et al. (2023) observed a similar pattern of ΔE ∗, which increased from 6.2 to 50.3 for CD added FG-carrageenan composite films. Fig. 3 a shows the visual representation of the FG films containing BA and CDs. The FG film was completely transparent, whereas the BA contained films appeared bluish, mainly due to the color of anthocyanins. However, upon incorporation of CDs into the FG polymer matrix, the film color was darkened to blue-brown because the yellow color of CDs was combined with the blue color of BA. Films with added BA and 5% CDs exhibit more intense dark colors compared to other films. The color was uniform across all film formulations, indicating that BA and CDs were uniformly distributed throughout the film.

Films containing BA were more opaque in the visible region than FG films and had significantly reduced light transmittance (Table 2). However, the concentration of CDs (1–5%) did not significantly affect the opacity (p > 0.05). The transparency of different films to UV and visible light is shown in Fig. 3b. The FG film was completely transparent in the visible light region, showing a light transmittance of more than 80%. However, all films exhibited effective UV-blocking properties due to the existence of aromatic amino acids (Alias & Mhd Sarbon, 2019). The inclusion of CDs led to a further decrease in light transmittance in a dose-dependent manner. The CDs (3% and 5%) integrated films suppressed nearly 99% of light in the UV region. The UV-A and UV-B light-blocking abilities of the films are shown in Fig. 3c. The CD-integrated film showed better UV-blocking ability compared to FG and FG/BA films. UV blocking increased with increasing CD concentration, while the 5% CD-containing film showed the highest blocking (98.4%). Additionally, films containing CDs exhibited better UV-B blocking ability compared to other films. No differences in UV-B protection (99%) were observed at different levels of CDs. Previously, Khan, Ezati, et al. (2023) observed UV-A blocking of ∼90.2% and UV-B blocking of 99.7% for CS-FG films containing green tea CDs. These findings showed that the inclusion of CDs in the FG film matrix exhibited the excellent UV-blocking properties, preventing discoloration and oxidation of photosensitive products (Uthirakumar, Devendiran, Kim, & Lee, 2018).

FG films containing BA and different CD concentrations were examined for surface and cross-sectional morphologies as shown in Fig. 4 . All films had a homogeneous, smooth, continuous, intact and crack-free surface morphology. SEM images showed the compatibility of film components. These findings showed that CDs were uniformly distributed across the film surface (Fig. 4a). Cross-sectional images of the FG and FG/BA films show intact morphology without voids or tears, demonstrating the compatibility of FG and BA at the molecular level (Fig. 4b). There were no clearly visible cracks, and the film surface exhibited exemplary integrity. Hydrogen bonds may occur between the hydroxyl groups of CD, BA, and FG (Khan, Priyadarshi, et al., 2023). However, the presence of CDs in the FG/BA/3%CD film showed small particle clusters, and these results were similar to previous findings (Roy & Rhim, 2021). Moreover, the FG/BA/5%CD film showed layer formation, and the layers were tightly aligned without any voids, potentially leading to higher TS and lower EAB of this film. Based on the above results, the inclusion of CDs could improve the stability and miniaturization of FG films, affecting several properties.

FTIR of FG-based films was performed to investigate the mode of interactions between the surface functional groups of FG, BA, and CDs (Fig. 4c). FTIR of BA showed a broad characteristic major peak of approximately 3292 cm−1 due to the presence of OH group and high-intensity peak around 1038 cm−1 represented C-O group. The peaks around 1608 and 1394 cm−1 corresponded with the stretching of C=O and aromatic alkanes, respectively (Mary, Koshy, Pillai, Reghunadhan, & Pothen, 2024). A broad absorption peak around 3300 to 3400 cm−1, indicating the stretching of -OH and N-H vibrations of FG, was observed (Nilsuwan, Guerrero, de la Caba, Benjakul, & Prodpran, 2020). In the BA-incorporated FG films, the peaks became broader and larger due to the coupling action of the amide-A group of FG and -OH group of BA (Yong et al., 2019). Each spectrum showed the distinctive absorption peaks of FG around 1656, 1550, and 1245 cm⁻1, corresponding to the amide I, II, and III of carbon chains, respectively (Khan, Ezati, et al., 2023). The attributed peaks in the range of 1060–1070 cm−1 were ascribed to the C-O stretching vibrations (Roy & Rhim, 2021). The slight variation in the amplitude of peaks was seen in IR spectra at lower wavenumbers associated with amino and carbonyl moieties after the addition of CDs, suggesting that electrostatic interactions were the primary interaction between these groups (Perera, Jaiswal, & Jaiswal, 2022).

Moreover, the FTIR spectra of films containing CS-CDs had a broader peak at 3200–3500 cm⁻1 and observed slight peak shifts, suggesting the interaction between some functional groups in FG film. These observations suggested that FG/BA/CD complex formation was influenced by interactions between the -OH groups of FG and CDs, either van der Waals interactions or H-bonds (Chen, Duan, Yu, & Xie, 2021). The FTIR spectra of the films containing BA and CDs were similar to those of the FG films except for slight changes in the positions and amplitudes of the peaks. The results confirmed the interaction of CDs in the FG/BA complex. Following the incorporation of CDs, the FTIR spectra displayed the bending of C-H, -OH, C-C, and C-O stretching vibrations. Moreover, the FTIR spectrum showed a slight shift in the frequency domain, indicating that the CDs were homogeneously integrated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS and EDX spectra of the films
ce:section.ce:para: The bonding arrangement and chemical composition of the FG and FG/BA/5%CD films were determined from the XPS pattern. As anticipated for the fabricated films, XPS spectra showed the major constitutional elements of the composite matrices. The spectra showed the occurrence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen with peaks indicated by the binding energies at 284.1, 398.9, and 532.6 eV for the FG film and at 286.1, 402.2, 532.6 eV for the FG/BA/5%CD film, respectively (Fig. 5 a). The high-resolution C 1s spectra of the FG and FG/BA/5%CD films showed four slitted peaks, with the bonding groups of C-C/C-H corresponding to the binding energies at 284.3 eV and 284.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 5b). The other split peaks at 285.1, 286.3, and 287.4 eV for FG and 285.6, 287.8, and 288.8 eV for FG/BA/5%CD indicated the bonding structures of C=C/C-N, C-O, and carbonyl (C=O) or O-C-O, respectively (Khan, Riahi, et al., 2023). Additionally, the three split peaks for N 1s spectra indicating the existence of C-N-C, N-C, and N-H/N-(CH3)3 + groups were attributed to the binding energies of 398.9, 399.5, and 400.2 eV for the FG film and 399.6, 400.1, and 401.2 eV for the FG/BA/5%CD film, respectively (Fig. 5c) (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023; Riahi, Khan, Rhim, Shin, & Kim, 2024). In addition, Fig. 5d displays O 1s spectra by splitting into three peaks at binding energies of ∼530.5, 531.5, and 532.3 eV for the FG film and 531.4, 532.4, and 533.5 eV for the FG/BA/5%CD film, respectively, validating the formation of C-O-C/C-O-H, C=O, and O-C-O bonds (Zhang et al., 2015). The slight shift in the binding energy of the separated slit peaks between the FG and FG/BA/5%CD films confirmed the successful insertion of BA and CDs as functional fillers into the FG polymer matrix.

The elemental components were assessed using EDX spectra for the FG and FG/BA/5%CD films (Fig. 5e and f). The results indicated that the primary elements in the film matrix were carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen. Carbon was constituted as the most dominant component. The FG/BA/5%CD films showed carbon at a percentage of 50.99, which was higher than that of FG films (49.81%). The scans revealed that the elements were uniformly distributed across the surface, indicating that homogenous dispersion of CDs across the FG polymer matrix. Khan, Priyadarshi, et al. (2023) also observed a similar distribution of green tea CDs over the CS/FG film surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and antimicrobial properties of the films
ce:section.ce:para: The DPPH and ABTS-RSA of the FG-based films were assessed, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 a. The FG film did not show notable antioxidant activity, but the CD-incorporated films showed excellent antioxidant properties. In particular, films containing more than 3% of CDs showed ABTS-RSA of almost 99%. The RSA of these films may result from proton transfer or hydrogen ion transfer from the functionalized groups of CDs on the surface (Murru, Badía-Laíño, & Díaz-García, 2020). Films with BA exhibited significant RSA due to the presence of free -OH groups of anthocyanins in BA (Tuan Putra, Zainol, MohdIsa, & MohdMaidin, 2021). Similar to the CDs, the RSA of the composite film was found to be higher in the ABTS-RSA assay than in the DPPH-RSA assay because the hydrophilic film and CDs could more easily dissolve and diffuse in the ABTS aqueous solution.

The antimicrobial activity of the films was tested against E. coli and L. monocytogenes as shown in Fig. 6b and c. The FG and FG/BA films showed no significant antibacterial activity because gelatin and anthocyanins do not have intrinsic antibacterial properties (Khan, Ezati, et al., 2023). However, the film containing CDs displayed excellent antibacterial efficacy, and as the concentration of CDs increased, the antibacterial activity also increased. In particular, films containing BA and 5% CDs showed excellent antibacterial activity and almost completely inhibited the growth of L. monocytogenes after 6 h of incubation. Overall, the CD-containing films showed stronger antibacterial activity against GPB (L. monocytogenes) than against GNB (E. coli) due to differences in the lipid layer of their cell wall (Fu et al., 2022). Undoubtedly, the antibacterial effectiveness of the nanocomposite film was mainly due to the antibacterial attributes of the CDs (Fig. 2b). These results might be correlated with the CDs having a small size, which was advantageous for effective penetration into the cell matrix. As a result, these CDs disrupted protein and nucleic acid synthesis and cell membrane permeability (Beg et al., 2017; Sheta, El-Sheikh, & Abd-Elzaher, 2018; Zheng et al., 2021). Additionally, the CDs can trigger the production of ROS and facilitate the release of intracellular components, ultimately leading to cell death (Beg et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color response of films toward various pHs, ammonia and acid vapors
ce:section.ce:para: The color changeability of the film was affected by different pHs, ammonia and acid vapors and the visual response of the film is shown in Fig. 7 pH is an important factor in determining food quality and has been used to indicate ammonia levels, which determine the quality of packaged foods. The film's color change was evaluated using buffers of various pH values and compared to its original color (control), and the results are shown in Fig. 7a. Alteration in the anthocyanin structure of BA cause color changes. The flavylium cation (red) is formed primarily under strongly acidic conditions (pH < 4). At pH 5–6, two colorless structures are formed: carbinol pseudobase and chalcone. Blue/purple quinonoid anhydrous bases (∼pH 6–8) and bright yellow or colorless chalcones (pH > 8) are formed at various pHs, making them a natural pH indicator (Choi, Lee, Lacroix, & Han, 2017; Ibrahim, Mahmoud, Osman, Abd El-Aal, & Eid, 2011). The transition from the initial color of the BA-containing films to red was dominated by the conversion of anthocyanins to flavylium cations when the films were exposed to acidic pH (2–6). In contrast, the solution changed color from purple/blue (indicating a carbinol pseudo-base) at neutral pH to green (indicating a chalcone form) at alkaline pH (Roy & Rhim, 2021). There was a clear difference in the color intensity of BA-containing films with additional CDs, and the difference increased with increasing CD concentration. The color of the film changed noticeably as the pH level shifted from acidic to alkaline. The observation was consistent with previous reports that other anthocyanin-rich polymer based films were sensitive to pH, as indicated by color changes (Roy & Rhim, 2021).

The film's sensitivity level in response to ammonia and acid vapors was monitored as shown in Fig. 7b. The FG film showed no difference in color for both acetic acid and ammonia vapor. Films containing BA upon the exposure to ammonia vapor turned a green shade due to the formation of BA in the form of chalcone (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2024). However, no significant differences in film color were observed for acetic acid vapor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of films to monitor shrimp quality
ce:section.ce:para: A storage experiment was carried out on shrimp under accelerated conditions to investigate the ability of the film to monitor the color change according to deterioration in shrimp quality during storage. The film was cut into 2 cm × 2 cm sizes, affixed to the inside of a container containing shrimp samples, and stored at 28 °C for 24 h to observe the color change of the film (Fig. 7c). The FG/BA film color was changed to green after 24 h, which was related to the green color of the film applied to the container in response to alkaline pH (Fig. 7a). For films containing the CDs, at high CD content, the a∗ and b∗ values of the formed films rose towards the positive axis and tended to obtain a darker green color, which was mainly caused by the brown color of the CDs. The levels of greenness indicated the degree of decomposition of the shrimp, and the change in the film color was caused by the production of volatile basic compounds that interacted with BA. Overall, a color change to green was detected in all films regardless of the level of CDs after 24 h of storage. However, since the brownish-yellow color could mask the green color of the film, it might be difficult to see the masked green change in films containing 5% CD.

During the storage at 28 °C, shrimp deteriorated at a faster rate. Consequently, significant quantities of volatile basic compounds like trimethylamine and ammonia were generated (Liu et al., 2021). Okpala, Choo, and Dykes (2014) reported the formation of total volatile base (TVB) in L. vannamei kept at 4 °C for 12 days. The initial TVB value upon storage was 9.94 mg/100 g and reached 38.30 mg/100 g at the end of storage. A TVB content of 30–35 mg/100 g is considered the daily intake associated with bacterial spoilage (FAO) (Connell, 1975). Abdillah, Lin, & Charles (2022) also found that the TVB of Pacific white shrimp was elevated when kept at room temperature rather than at refrigerated storage. This upsurge in TVB content was correlated with the higher pH of Pacific white shrimp during long-term storage at 4 °C (Liu et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quality changes and real-time monitoring of Pacific white shrimps
ce:section.ce:para: Pacific white shrimp were packaged in pouches made from FG-based films along with a pouch of polyethylene (PE) film for comparison and quality changes of shrimp were investigated over 12 days of storage at 4 °C. The images recorded by visual monitoring at 3-day intervals are shown in Fig. 8 . Film pouches containing BA showed a visibly color change from blue to green during storage. Among all pouches, the pouch made with BA-doped film exhibited a more vivid color transition from blue to green than the other pouches. For pouches made from films containing higher levels of the CDs, the brown color of the films might mask obvious changes in anthocyanin color affected by volatile bases associated with elevated pH. The intensity of color change varied based on the storage period and CD concentration. In the case of pouches containing CDs, as the concentration of CD increased, the degree of color change appeared slowly, and the speed of change also decreased. The spoilage of shrimp might be delayed due to the RSA and antibacterial activities of CDs. These changes might occur due to the antibacterial and barrier properties of CDs distributed in the film. As a result, corruption could be curbed to a significant degree (Roy et al., 2021). Therefore, these pouches could be utilized to prolong the shelf life of packaged shrimp with real-time quality monitoring for consumers through color changes.

The quality attributes of shrimp packed in different pouches, such as pH, redness, TBARS value, TVC and PBC, were determined at 3-day intervals for 12 days stored at 4 °C. The quality attributes of the shrimps packed in formulated pouches, such as pH, redness (a∗-value), yellowness (b∗-value), TBARS and microbiological quality (TVC and PBC) during storage of 12 days are shown in Fig. 9 . The pH of the shrimp samples upsurged from 6.9 to 9.6, 9.3, 9.1, 8.8, 8.4 and 8.3 when packed in PE, FG, FG/BA, FG/BA/1%CD, FG/BA/3%CD and FG/BA/5%CD pouches, respectively, on day 12 of storage (Fig. 9a). Shrimp samples packaged in pouches made with films containing CDs had lower pH, especially those packaged in pouches made with 5% CD films until the end of storage. The increase in pH in shrimp meat is due to microbial degradation of nitrogen compounds, which is associated with the alkaline pH of shrimp samples (Liu et al., 2021).

As the storage period increased, the condition of the shrimp began to deteriorate, and the surface color of the shrimp meat shifted from gray to yellow. The red and yellow colors of the shrimp indicated rotten shrimp (Fan, Schneider, & Sarnoski, 2022). Therefore, changes in a∗ and b∗-values confirmed those changes (Fig. 9b and c). During the storage period, a∗ and b∗-values varied, depending on the samples kept in different pouches. The shrimp kept in pouches made from CD incorporated films showed lower intensity of a∗ and b∗-values, compared to those without CDs and commercial PE. After 12 days of storage, the a∗-value of the shrimp packed in PE and FG pouches exhibited high values around 3.73, whereas the shrimp samples stored in a pouch made from film containing 1%, 3%, and 5% CDs had a∗-values of 2.04, 1.93 and 1.46, respectively. Compared to shrimp packed in PE and FG pouches, the b∗-value of shrimp packed with pouches of FG film with 3% and 5% CD showed low values of 4.07 and 3.06, respectively, while the initial value was 6.9.

The TBARS values of the shrimps in the mg MDA equivalent/kg sample (Liu et al., 2021) during storage for 12 days are shown in Fig. 9d. TBARS values are commonly used as an indicator to assess the extent of secondary lipid oxidation products and elevated MDA content is linked with the spoilage of the products (Franklin & Mitchell, 2019). For samples packaged in PE pouches and pouches made from FG and FG/BA films, MDA values reached up to 1.4 mg MDA equivalent/kg of sample. In fact, samples preserved in pouches containing CDs exhibited low TBARS values ranging from 0.65 to 1 mg MDA equivalent/kg sample, indicating that pouches made from FG films containing CDs inhibited lipid oxidation. Among them, the pouch made from a film containing 5% CDs showed a more powerful effect, and these results were consistent with the antioxidant activity of the CDs (Fig. 2a).

Microbial counts such as TVC and PBC of packaged shrimp stored at 4 °C have been used to monitor the quality (Temdee, Singh, & Benjakul, 2022). TVC and PBC of shrimp during storage at 4 °C are shown in Fig. 9e and f. The TVC and PBC of shrimp increased steadily over time. Initially, TVC and PBC were 3.00 and 2.23 log CFU/mL, respectively. Shrimp packaged in PE pouches and shrimp packed with FG film without CDs showed increases in TVC and PBC of approximately 10 and 7 log CFU/mL, respectively, on day 12 of storage. However, shrimp packaged in pouches containing CDs showed values lower than the maximum permissible limit (6 log CFU/mL) (Mohebi & Shahbazi, 2017). Pouches made with the FG/BA/5%CD film exhibited the lowest TVC and PBC (4.9 and 4.4 log CFU/mL, respectively) among other pouches made with CD-containing films. These results reaffirmed that pouches made of FG/BA/5%CD film could effectively inhibit microbial growth and extend the freshness of shrimp for more than 12 days.

Hydrothermally produced blue-emitting CDs were incorporated into FG films containing BA to generate multifunctional properties. The obtained CDs had the size of the quantum domain and was compatible with the FG polymer matrix along with BA, resulting in a uniform film. These CD-integrated films had excellent antibacterial and antioxidant properties along with improved mechanical, water vapor barrier and UV protection properties. The FG/BA films containing CDs showed a good response to color changes associated with shrimp quality deterioration. Color changes were closely related to changes in shrimp pH, TBARS content, and microbial load. Shrimp samples packaged in pouches made of CDs supplemented with FG-based multifunctional films maintained their color with reduced rancidity and microbial growth up to 12 days stored at 4 °C. In particular, pouches made from FG/BA/5%CD films exhibited excellent preservation properties with significant color variation depending on shrimp quality. Therefore, the FG/BA/CD films could be used in active and intelligent packaging applications to preserve shrimp vulnerable to lipid oxidation and microbial degradation while providing real-time quality indicators of stored shrimp.

Arunachalasivamani Ponnusamy: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ajahar Khan: Supervision, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Thummanoon Prodpran: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Jong-Whan Rhim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.
